sanskrit_devanagari:
यामिमां पुष्पितां वाचं प्रवदन्त्यविपश्चितः ।
वेदवादरताः पार्थ नान्यदस्तीति वादिनः ॥ ४२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yām imāṁ puṣpitāṁ vācaṁ pravadanty avipaścitaḥ |
veda-vāda-ratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ || 42 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; vedabase combines 2.42 and 2.43 into a single page because Ramanuja's bhāṣya treats them as one syntactic unit; verse 42 hemistichs extracted from the combined block; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; the page also presents 2.42-43 together but verse 42 is independently cited; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.42।।)
IAST exact (the page combines 2.42 with 2.43 in a single URL; verse 42 hemistichs match: 'yām imāṁ puṣpitāṁ vācaṁ pravadanty avipaścitaḥ veda-vāda-ratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.042). IAST body identical: 'yām imāṃ puṣpitāṃ vācaṃ pravadanty avipaścitaḥ / vedavādaratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ'. Anusvara-vs-assimilated-nasal orthographic variant only (saṃ vs sam, etc.).

anvaya.meaning: which (acc. fem. sg., agreeing with vācam)
devoted to the words of the Vedas (specifically the kāmya-karma portions)
O son of Pṛthā (vocative addressing Arjuna)

translations.verbatim_capture_note: combined 2.42-43 verbatim quote; 2.42 covered by clauses up to 'they say that there is nothing more than this'
translations.verbatim_quote: Men of small knowledge are very much attached to the flowery words of the Vedas, which recommend various fruitive activities for elevation to heavenly planets, resultant good birth, power, and so forth. Being desirous of sense gratification and opulent life, they say that there is nothing more than this.

translations.verbatim_capture_note: combined 2.42-43 verbatim quote; 2.42 covered by the first sentence
translations.verbatim_quote: Those with limited understanding, get attracted to the flowery words of the Vedas, which advocate ostentatious rituals for elevation to the celestial abodes, and presume no higher principle is described in them.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt of 1978-char combined 2.42-43 purport; 2.42 anchor)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: People in general are not very intelligent, and due to their ignorance they are most attached to the fruitive activities recommended in the karma-kāṇḍa portions of the Vedas. They do not want anything more than sense gratificatory proposals for enjoying life in heaven, where wine and women are available and material opulence is very common.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.42 Partha, O son of Prtha; those devoid of one-pointed conviction, who pravadanti, utter; imam, this; yam puspitam vacam, flowery talk, which is going to be stated, which is beautiful like a tree in bloom, pleasant to hear, and appears to be (meaningful) sentences; — who are they? they are — avipascitah, people who are undiscerning, of poor intellect; veda-vada-ratah, who remain engrossed in the utterances of the Vedas, in the Vedic sentences which reveal many panegyrics, fruits of action and their means.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara is precise about scope: it is not the Vedas that are being criticised but those who are veda-vāda-ratāḥ — engrossed specifically in the utterances of the Vedas that reveal panegyrics and fruits of action (the kāmya-karma portion). They declare 'na anyat asti' (nothing else exists) because their reading is restricted to those passages. The flowery (puṣpita) speech is named as a tree in bloom: visually beautiful, tonally pleasing, syntactically well-formed sentences — yet, when the discriminating reader checks for self-knowledge content, the flowers do not bear fruit. Shankara explicitly preserves the rest of the Veda's authority (the upaniṣadic / jñāna-kāṇḍa portion) — the critique scopes to the kāmya fixation.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use; Ramanuja treats 2.42, 2.43 and 2.44 as a single unit — excerpt is the joint 2.42-44 bhāṣya, 2.42 portion)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.42 – 2.44 The ignorant, whose knowledge is little, and who have as their sole aim the attainment of enjoyment and power, speak the flowery language i.e., having its flowers (show) only as fruits, which look apparently beautiful at first sight. They rejoice in the letter of the Vedas i.e., they are attached to heaven and such other results (promised in the Karma-kanda of the Vedas). They say that there is nothing else, owing to their intense attachment to these results.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja explicitly treats 2.42-2.44 as one syntactic unit. The 'flowery language' is so named because what looks like fruit at first sight is in fact only flower — visual promise without nutritive substance. The avipaścitaḥ rejoice in the letter (vāda) of the Vedas because they are intensely attached to the karma-kāṇḍa results (svarga, opulence, sense-gratification). The 'na anyat asti' is read as a consequence of attachment, not as a metaphysical claim — the flowery speakers have foreclosed any other goal because their attachment makes other goals invisible to them. The Vedas themselves remain authoritative; what is criticised is the partial, attachment-driven reading.

positions.rendering: supposed followers of the Vedas; advocates who say there is nothing else
those who presume no higher principle is described in (the Vedas)
engrossed specifically in Vedic utterances of panegyric and fruits of action; declare 'nothing else exists' because their reading is restricted
rejoice in the letter of the Vedas because attached to heaven; declare 'nothing else exists' as a consequence of attachment

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse is doctrinally load-bearing because of how the critique is scoped. Prabhupada's 'supposed followers of the Vedas' reads sharp — implying these people misclaim Vedic authority; Mukundananda's 'presume no higher principle is described in them' is gentler and loses some of Krishna's sting. Shankara and Ramanuja both restrict the critique to the kāmya-karma reading: the Vedas are not at fault; the partial-reading-driven-by-attachment is. The verse is therefore not anti-Vedic — it is anti-fixation-on-one-portion-of-the-Vedas. Modern flat translations risk reading as 'the Vedas are flowery and false', which neither traditional commentator accepts. The locus of the critique is the avipaścitaḥ (the undiscerning), not the texts.

literal_meaning: This flowery speech that the unwise utter — devoted to the words of the Vedas, O Pārtha, declaring 'nothing else exists' — (continued in 2.43).
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a class of speakers — the avipaścitaḥ, the undiscerning — who utter puṣpita-vāc, flowery (literally: in-bloom) speech. They are veda-vāda-rata, devoted to the words of the Vedas — but the unanimous commentarial reading scopes this to the kāmya-karma portion (the desire-driven ritual prescriptions). Their characteristic claim is 'na anyat asti' — there is nothing else (beyond ritual fruits like svarga, opulence, sense-enjoyment). Both Shankara and Ramanuja insist the critique is of fixation, not of the Vedas: the Vedas contain jñāna-kāṇḍa (knowledge-section) and karma-kāṇḍa (action-section), and to read only the latter — and to read it for fruit-collection — is the pathology being named. The verse forms a syntactic unit with 2.43 (and Ramanuja extends to 2.44): the relative pronoun 'yām' (which) launches the description that 2.43 completes.

cross_references.explanation: 2.42 introduces the relative-clause subject (yām imāṁ puṣpitāṁ vācaṁ); 2.43 supplies the predicate (kāmātmānaḥ svarga-parā ... bhogaiśvarya-gatiṁ prati). The two verses must be read together. Ramanuja extends the unit to 2.44.

2.46 ('the small water-pond before the flood') closes the same arc that 2.42-45 opens: the Vedas are scoped, the realised one's relation to ritual is reframed. The arc is not anti-Vedic; it is anti-exclusive-fixation.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr., joint 2.42-44 bhāṣya)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja treats 2.42-44 as one bhāṣya unit; the 2.42-only excerpt is the opening segment, not an independent commentary.
